text: Alright, listen up. {mark} — villa on the hill, three floors, old money. Security grade {security}, which means a gate, cameras on the garden side, and a guard who does his rounds like clockwork. Every room in there is hiding something worth carrying out. The owner's at the opera. We have the run of the place — for now.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Eyes open. {mark}. Beautiful property — shame about what we're going to do to its insurance premium. Villa layout, two stairwells, service entrance on a separate circuit. The money's spread through the whole house — how much of it leaves with us tonight is entirely up to you.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Here's the mark: {mark}. Owner thinks a gate and a night guard makes it a fortress. It makes it a queue. There's a payday behind every door in there; security reads {security} on my sheet, which I'd call optimistic. Theirs, not ours.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Listen up. {mark} — a proper estate, the kind with its own chapel and a wine cellar nobody's read the inventory on. Grounds patrol, dogs on the east lawn, and more rooms than the staff can keep an eye on. Security grade {security}. We go room by room and carry out what we can.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

The mark is {mark}. Estates like this were built to keep peasants out, not us. Long corridors, staff doors everywhere, and a take spread across more rooms than they can watch. {security}-grade security and a groundskeeper who's mostly asleep. Mostly.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Here we go: {mark}. Old family, old money, old locks — and one very new alarm system they bought after the last crew got greedy. The last crew left empty-handed. We're better than the last crew. Prove me right.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Top of the city: {mark}. Penthouse job — one way in, one way out, and forty floors of consequences underneath us. Concierge, key-card lifts, {security}-grade cameras. Everything up there is small, expensive, and ours if we keep our nerve. The view's free.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

The mark is {mark}, and it's a penthouse, which means everything is small, expensive, and alarmed. The lift logs every card. The stairs add eight minutes. The clock starts the moment the lobby cameras blink — every minute up there is heat on our necks.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.

Look up — {mark}, the penthouse. Owner collects art, watches, and enemies. We're here for the first two. Security grade {security}, and a doorman who notices shoes. Walk like you're rich and follow my voice.

Here's the shape of the night. We go room by room. Every room I'll show you two ways through — a quiet take and a greedy take. You pick the door together, then you tell me exactly who's going in. The people you send do the work; everyone else holds their breath.

The jobs themselves happen right there on your table — cards, dice, steady hands, fast mouths. I'll referee from the van and keep score. Every room pays out — loot, gear, or both — and heat builds the longer we stay; at twenty we're running. How fat the bag gets is down to how long you push. Everybody ready? Then we breach on my mark.
Room {roomNum}. Stay tight, stay quiet — {mark} has no idea we're here yet.
I've got two ways through this one on the feed. Read both doors out loud, pick one as a table, then tap in who's doing the job.
Next room — {crew}, stack up. This is what we trained for.
Same drill as always: two doors, one call. The quiet take keeps the heat down; the greedy take pays for the holiday. Choose, then send your people.
Moving to room {roomNum}. The bag's getting heavier — keep your heads.
Look at the two options, weigh the heat, and commit a crew. Whoever plays this room sits the next one out, so spend your specialists wisely.
Here we go. {crew}, on my signal.
Doors are up. Big payout means big exposure — your call. Pick the door, pick the people, and I'll set the difficulty from who you send.
Room {roomNum}, and the floor plan says this one's interesting.
Two ways in. Talk it over — out loud, that's allowed — then commit. Remember: the right specialist makes a hard job easy. I'll show you.
Through here, room {roomNum}. I can hear your heartbeats from the van. I like it.
Pick your door. Then pick your players — tap them in on my console and watch the dial move. Stats matter, people.
Room {roomNum}. The {security} security in this place is starting to wake up, so let's be crisp.
Two doors, one decision. Greedy pays more and burns more heat. When you've chosen, name who's going in.
Okay — room {roomNum}. Smells like furniture polish and poor decisions in there.
Read both doors to the table. Whoever's freshest takes point; the played-out ones rest. Commit when ready.
{crew} — room {roomNum} is live.
You know the rhythm by now: door, crew, job. The table does the work, I keep the score. Take your pick.
One more door, one more payday. Room {roomNum}.
Options are on screen. Quiet or greedy — and before you choose, look at who's rested. The next room won't wait for tired hands.
Room {roomNum}. We're past the easy part — everything from here is earned.
Two takes on the table. Choose one, commit your crew, and I'll deal you the job.
Slow breath. Room {roomNum}.
Here are your doors. The greedy line is shinier — it always is. Decide together, then send the right people for the lane.
Hold up — room {roomNum} isn't a job, it's a situation. I'm seeing it on the feed.
No doors to pick here. Something's happened and the table has to decide what we do about it. Listen close.
Room {roomNum}. Huh. That's not on the floor plan.
This one's a judgement call, not a lock. Hear it out, argue it round the table, and own whatever you choose.
Easy, easy — something's moving in room {roomNum}. Nobody breathe.
It's a situation, not a job. The decision is yours and the consequences are hidden until you commit. Welcome to crime.
{crew}, eyes up. Room {roomNum} just got interesting.
There's no clean way to plan for this one. Two calls on my screen, no prices on either. Talk it out.
Room {roomNum}. The night's throwing us a curveball.
Forget stats for a second — this is a table decision. Read it aloud, weigh it, commit together.
Wait — I've got chatter on the scanner about room {roomNum}. Something's up.
Nothing to deal, nothing to time. Just a choice with teeth. Make it as a crew.
Room {roomNum}, and my feed says: complication.
This is the part of the job no one rehearses. Two ways to play it, both blind. Decide and live with it.
Door's open on room {roomNum}. What's inside isn't a lock — it's a problem.
Judgement call. The smart move and the brave move are rarely the same one. Pick yours.
Room {roomNum}. I'd tell you what to do here, but I'm in a van and you're in the building.
Read both calls to the table, argue your case, and commit. No take-backs once you do.
Heads together, crew — room {roomNum} needs a decision, not a specialist.
Whatever you pick, pick it loud and pick it as one. The consequences come after the commitment.
That lock's a tumbler stack — the pins only seat in rising order, and they seat by feel, not by sight.
Table job: I'll have you deal cards to the crew going in. Nobody talks, nobody shows a hand. You play them to the table lowest to highest — one wrong drop and the whole stack screams.
Hear that? Neither do I. That's the problem — this lock gives you nothing. You work it blind, together, in order.
Cards face-down to the players going in. Eyes on your own. Read the room, time your play, rising order only. Equal pins can stack — that's your one mercy.
Someone put a beautiful lock in this door, and beautiful locks are vain — they want a slow, confident touch.
Each of you gets cards only you may see. The table plays them out in ascending order without a word. Trust your gut on who's holding low.
Tumblers. Old school. The kind of lock that punishes impatience and rewards a crew that can read each other.
Deal the cards, keep them private, play them rising. If two of you lock eyes and nobody moves — someone's holding the low card and doesn't know it.
The corridor camera sweeps on a fixed cycle. I can play you the rhythm — then it goes quiet and you carry the count in your head.
Listen to the beat, keep counting after it cuts out, and slap the table dead on the target. I'll be timing the slap to the millisecond.
Pressure plate on a timer. The interval's regular as a heartbeat — until the sound drops and it's all internal.
One player takes this: count with the metronome, keep counting through the silence, hit the table on the number. Early is a tripwire, late is a guard.
The guard's rounds run on the building's clock, and the building's clock ticks louder than he thinks.
I'll give you the tempo. When it mutes, the count lives in your chest. Slap the table on the target beat — I'll tell the room how close you were.
Electronic lock with a timing window. Machines don't get nervous. Let's see if you do.
Count the beats — aloud at first, silent after the mute — and strike the table exactly on the mark. The van hears everything.
Cocktail party two rooms over, and you're about to walk straight through it. The only way past small talk is more small talk.
Whole table plays. I give you a category, you rattle off answers around the room before the clock dies. Hit the count and nobody remembers your faces.
There's a doorman ahead who loves a chat. We're going to drown him in charm.
Everyone in — I'll read the category, you shout answers, I'll count the good ones. Hesitate too long and the smile slips.
Security's doing voice checks at this door. The trick isn't sounding right — it's never stopping talking.
One category, the whole table answering in turn, against the clock. Repeats don't count. Blanks definitely don't count.
You'll need patter for this one. Patter, and the ability to never, ever dry up.
Table game: category drops, answers fly, I tally. Reach the target before the buzzer and we glide through.
This room is wired to notice when things move. So you're going to notice first, and notice better.
I'll have a row of cards laid out — study every detail while the clock runs. Then you look away, the room changes, and you tell me exactly what moved.
The display cases in here get rearranged by the night staff. Tonight, the night staff is us.
Memorise the row — order, values, suits. After the screen drops, something will be different. Call out what changed. Be precise.
Trust nothing in this room. Especially not your own eyes — unless you've trained them.
Study the spread while you can. Changes are coming the moment you look away. Spot every one of them and we're ghosts.
A thief's memory is a camera. Time to find out what resolution yours runs at.
Eyes on the cards until the timer ends. Then eyes shut while the room shifts. Then tell me what's wrong with the picture.
Junction box ahead. I'm patching its keypad feed straight to my console — I'll turn the screen so you can see it.
Watch the pads light up in sequence, then tap the same sequence back. One wrong pad and the circuit bites.
Four relays, one safe path through them, and the path changes every time someone re-arms it.
The console will flash you the order. Hold it in your head and play it back, tap for tap. Speed matters less than certainty.
The alarm loop in this wall is musical, in its way. It has a melody. Learn it.
Watch the pattern on my screen, then repeat it. The chain gets longer the deeper you go — break it early and we all hear about it.
Whoever wired this had rhythm. Respect it.
Lit pads, in order, then you echo them back on the screen. Fingers steady. The building is listening.
The floor manager talks shop with anyone who sounds like they belong. So tonight, you belong.
Whole table plays. I'll fire questions; you confer and answer fast. Enough right answers and the door opens itself.
There's a keypad here, but the real lock is the man standing next to it who likes to quiz strangers.
Rapid-fire trivia, everyone in. Argue, agree, answer — I'll mark each one. Hit the threshold before the clock.
I bought you a cover story: visiting consultants. Consultants know things. Time to know things.
Questions are coming. The table answers together. I keep the score and the room keeps its suspicions — if you're quick.
Knowledge is a skeleton key, and this floor runs on gossip and facts.
I read, you answer, the group mind does the work. Get enough right and we're invisible.
There it is. The safe. Combination dial, feedback clicks — it tells you how close you are, if you know how to listen.
Call out your guesses digit by digit; I'll type them in and read back the clicks: how many digits right, how many in the right place. Crack it before the tries run out.
This box has a tell. Every wrong combination, it confesses a little — right numbers, wrong slots.
Work the code as a team. Guess, read the feedback, narrow it down. You've got a handful of attempts and a clock with opinions.
The owner chose the combination. Owners are sentimental and lazy. The safe will tell you the rest.
Shout the guesses, I'll feed them in and give you the read-back. Every attempt buys information — spend them like they're money.
A safe is just a conversation where one side starts out lying.
Guesses in, feedback out — right digit, right place, or close-but-wrong. Deduce fast. The tries are limited and so is my patience.
This room's a quiet one — guard asleep down the hall, so not a sound. The loot sorts itself if you keep it moving.

Whole table, dead silent: I'll set the deck. On your count everyone slides one card left at the same time — fast. Four of a kind goes down and you're safe. Someone's left holding junk; that's just how it goes.
Thin walls in here. One word and the whole floor wakes up — so nobody says anything.
Everyone gets a hand, a couple of you get an extra bogus card. Pass left together, no talking, collect four of a kind and lay it down. Keep passing while you've still got a card.
Silent job. The fun part is watching you all try to organise without a single word.
Deal as I say — some cards are pure junk, can't make a set. Pass one card left on the beat, all at once, lay down your four. Tip-Off will name the real ranks if you're drowning.
Library-quiet, this one. Sorting the haul is easy; doing it without a peep is the trick.
No talking at all. On the count, one card left, everybody, every time. Four matching goes down. Beat the clock and don't be the one frozen holding the bogus card when it stops.
Motion sensors in the floor. The crossing's possible, but the counterweights need building — gently.
Build a card house to the target height before the timer dies. A tier is two leaning cards capped flat. Breathe through your nose. Don't sneeze.
The shelf above the sensor needs propping, and all we've got is what's in your hands.
Card tower, target height, ticking clock. If it topples, start again — the clock won't wait. Steady beats fast.
This is surgery with playing cards. The patient is a laser grid.
Tiers up to the target before the buzzer. The table judges what's standing. Extra Hands gets everyone in for ten glorious seconds — if someone's earned the shout.
I've seen demolition men with shakier hands than this crew. Don't make me take that back.
Build. Tier by tier, to the height I call. Standing at the buzzer is all that counts.
Alarm panel, live wires, and a manual I can only transmit to one earpiece. Of course it couldn't be simple.
One of you becomes the reader — they get the rules and must not see the cards. The rest see the wires and must not see the rules. Describe, decide, cut. Yes/no answers only between you.
The box is colour-coded, suit-coded, and booby-trapped. The disarm sheet is in my hands, and I can give it to exactly one of you.
Reader takes the manual screen and turns their back. Crew reads the row of wires out loud. The reader names the cuts; flip each named card face-down. One wrong cut ends the conversation.
You'll find a row of wires that look like playing cards. That's because the installer had a sense of humour. The manual doesn't.
Split duties: one reader with the rules, everyone else with eyes on the cards. Talk in card-speak — colour, suit, number. Cut everything the rules mark safe, touch nothing else.
Live alarm. Manual's encrypted to a single receiver. Pick your calmest voice to hold it.
The reader hears descriptions, applies the rules, and calls the cuts. The crew flips the named wires face-down. When every matching wire is down, tell me 'all clear' — and be right.

optionDescription.text: Play it by the book. Slower, smaller, and nobody calls the police.
The careful approach. We take what we came for and leave the room looking bored.
Low and quiet. Less loot, less heat, longer career.
Standard procedure. No noise, no flourish, no stories to tell.
Take the certain win. The bonus stays on the table; so does our anonymity.
The professional's line: in, out, invisible.
Controlled. The kind of choice you make when you plan on a tomorrow.
Quiet hands, quiet exit. The meter barely moves.
We came for the job, not the jackpot. This is the job.
Modest take, clean conscience, cool heat. I sleep well either way.
More loot, more heat. That's the whole trade. Make the call.
The aggressive line. Big take, big exposure — worth it if you're good enough.
Push your luck. The upside is real and so is the cost of failure.
Double down. The payout's there; so are the consequences.
All in on the big take. Just know what you're signing up for.
The greedy call. Higher loot, higher heat — always has been.
Every run has one door like this. Question is whether this crew opens it.
The shiny option. Shiny attracts attention. Your move.
Take the bonus, feed the meter. The van will be running either way.
Rich or famous — this door offers both, and you only want one of them.

text: Hold on — something's changed in here. I'm seeing it on the feed.
This one's a judgement call, not a job. Read both options to the table, argue it out, pick one. The consequences stay hidden until you commit — no take-backs.
Okay, slight complication. Not a crisis — a situation.
Two ways to play it. Talk it through together and choose. Some of these need a roll from whoever attempts it, so think about who's got the stats.
Live one, people. I'm watching it happen on the cameras right now.
Make a call as a crew. If the choice needs nerve, you'll pick one person to attempt it — their lane rating sweetens the roll. Choose like it matters, because it does.
Something just landed in your lap.
Here's the decision. Both doors are blind — you'll know what it cost when it's already spent. Read them aloud and commit.
Van to crew: opportunity or trap ahead. Possibly both. Usually both.
Weigh the options. If a roll is needed, send your strongest in that lane — resting crew can't attempt. Then we live with it.
Freeze. Look left. You see it too?
Decision time. No dice yet, just nerve: pick an option together, then I'll tell you if fate wants a roll.

outcomeQuip.text: Textbook. That's what I like to see. Take your spoils and move.
Clean. Not a scratch on it. You made that look easy.
Zero alarms. I could kiss this whole crew. Don't stop.
Professional. That's the word I'd use. Also: move.
Invisible. They'll check this room for a week and find nothing but their own fingerprints.
That's how it's drawn up on the whiteboard. Nobody ever does it like the whiteboard.
Full take, no trace. Whatever you're being paid, it's not enough.
That was... fine. Not good. Fine. We're still standing and the bag's not empty.
Got it done — loudly. Minor alarm response, partial take. Keep the pace.
Half marks. You got the cash and woke the dog. Story of this business.
Scrappy. The kind of win you don't put on the highlight reel. It still counts.
They're checking, not chasing. We bank what we grabbed and we keep moving.
I've seen prettier. I've also seen worse, and that crew's doing eight years.
A wobble, not a fall. Pocket the partial and walk it off.
Alarm's live. No take from that one — move, move, we fix it on the fly.
That one cost us real ground. Heat's up, pockets aren't. Make the next one count.
Botched. Happens to every crew once a night. Once. You hear me?
Okay. Deep breath. The room won, the building knows something's wrong, and we are still in the game.
I'm not angry, I'm recalculating the escape route. Which is worse.
Loud, expensive, and empty-handed. The trifecta. Walk it off and listen for sirens.
We'll laugh about that one eventually. Tonight we run a little faster instead.

text: Good haul so far, and the building still thinks it's asleep.
So here's the offer, same as always: one more room for a bigger bag, or I bring the car around and we bank what we've got. Push on, or call the getaway — table decides.
Heat's barely a flicker. Honestly, this is embarrassing for them.
We could keep eating rooms all night at this rate — or take the easy win. Push deeper, or get out clean? Your call, crew.
Quietest job I've run in years. The meter's cold and the rooms keep coming.
Decision point: another room, more loot, a little more risk — or the exit while it's still a formality. What's it going to be?
Take stock. You've done well — and they've started noticing the doors you've left open.
The smart play might be the exit. The brave play is one more room. Smart or brave — pick one, out loud, together.
Heat's climbing past comfortable. Not blown — warm. Like a hand on the kettle.
One more room pushes our luck and pads the bag. The car is four minutes out either way. Push on, or call it?
We are drawing real attention now. Radios are talking to each other and none of them sound bored.
This is the moment, crew. Cut and run with what we're carrying, or gamble one more room against a building that's waking up. I need the call now.
Scanner says they're circling. The window's closing — fast enough that I've started the engine.
Every room from here is played against the clock and the law. One more, or out? Decide like professionals.
The fence is asking how much we're bringing. I told him 'more if they're brave, alive if they're smart.'
Your move: push on for the bigger take, or call the getaway and keep what's banked. No wrong answer — only consequences.
Alright — it's go time. Car's two blocks east, engine warm, and the only thing between you and it is every camera we've annoyed tonight.

Here's how the escape works: I deal clue cards, the clock runs, and you talk your way through them around the table — describer can say anything except the words on the card. Clear the target stack before the timer dies and we vanish.
Reader rotates every card. Loud is fine. Fast is mandatory. On my mark... go.
Getaway clock is live. Every second in this building is a second closer to a patrol car.
The escape's a relay: clue cards, round the table, beat the buzzer. Describe, guess, slam it down, next card. Skips cost us; ditching loot buys a pass — desperate measures only.
Heads up, mouths fast. Let's earn the second half of your cut.
We're in the endgame, people. I handle the route; you handle the speed.
Cards are coming. One of you describes, the table shouts guesses, every cleared card is a street we put behind us. Hit the target count before the clock and {mark} becomes a fond memory.
Three. Two. One — talk.
Time to leave. Smoothly, quickly, and all at once.
The run home is played as clues against the clock: clear the stack, rotate the describer, no flat tyres. If a card's impossible, skip it if we've got skips — or ditch loot to buy room. Ready?
That's the alarm threshold — we're done collecting, now we're escaping.
Same rules as every great exit: keep talking, keep guessing, keep moving. Target count's on my screen, clock's in my hand. Beat both.

getawayCountdown.text: Keep going — the car's right there. Don't look back, it slows the guessing.
Thirty seconds of corridor left. If something's going wrong, I don't want to hear it until we're driving.
Move, move, move. This is why we practised the exit. Nobody practised the exit, did they.
Nearly clear. Eyes forward, next card, go.
Sirens, two streets over and wrong about which two streets. Keep talking.
The fence is flashing his lights. That's either a greeting or a warning. Clear the stack and we won't care which.

text: That's a clean score. The kind you tell stories about — not tonight, but eventually, and with exaggerations.
Count it, split it, and somebody buy the van a drink. {mark} never knew what hit them.
We're out. Clean. The take is real and the heat is somebody else's problem now.
Final tally's on the screen. Read it aloud and take a bow, crew.

winSting.text: In the clear. Count the money later — right now just appreciate that you actually pulled it off.
Everybody out, loot in the bag, city none the wiser. That's the job, done properly.
And breathe. The score's banked, the trail's cold, and I already deleted the footage of the embarrassing parts.

text: The heat caught us at the line. The haul's lighter than it should be — but the crew's intact, and crews are harder to replace than cash.
Read the final tally, take the lesson, and start planning the next one. We owe this town a rematch.

bustSting.text: That's a bust. Still alive, still free — for now. Some nights, that's the whole prize.
We got sloppy or we got unlucky, and the ledger doesn't care which. Reduced take. Lessons all round.
Heat overrun. The score's clipped. I've had worse nights — not many, but enough to know we'll be fine.

text: They were waiting at the exit. We dumped speed for distance and it cost us a slice of the take.
Final numbers on screen. Lighter than we dreamed, heavier than zero. Same time next month?

scenarioReveal.text: That's the call made. {mark} is none the wiser — or very much wiser. Either way: move.
Decision made, consequences landing. The effect's on my screen — read it out and keep walking.
Done is done. Check the damage, pocket the windfall, onward.
And that's why I stay in the van. Result's up — announce it to the table.
The dice have opinions tonight. Noted. Next room's waiting.
Committed. Whatever that just cost or paid, it's ours now.
Filed under 'seemed like a good idea at the time'. Effect's revealed — let's roll.
That choice will either haunt us or fund us. The screen knows which. Read it.
Clean decision-making under pressure. Whatever happens next, I'm putting that in your references.
Outcome's locked in. The night's not done with us yet — eyes back on the job.
The room had a test and you answered it. Results on screen. Moving on.
No take-backs, remember. Read the effect, square your shoulders, next door.
